demographics: I am 55 years old. I am male. I am White. I am married. I completed high school (12th grade). My family income is $60,000 to $74,999. I am a strong Republican. I am extremely conservative. My religion is Protestant. I attend religious services more than once a week.

events.event_text: When I was 32, I lost my job of 10 years due to a company-wide layoff. I struggled to find new employment for over a year, and the strain led to my wife and I separating temporarily. Our church community rallied around us, providing financial aid and emotional support, which helped us weather the crisis together.

When I was 45, I was asked by our church's leadership to join a mission trip to help rebuild a community devastated by a natural disaster. Seeing firsthand the struggles of others, and how our faith could make a real difference, reinforced my beliefs and deepened my commitment to service.

When I was 40, my best friend of 25 years, who was Black, moved away suddenly due to a job relocation. Although we promised to keep in touch, the distance and our busy lives made it difficult, and eventually, we lost contact. I miss our shared history and the understanding that came with it.

When I was 42, my oldest son came out to me as gay. He'd always been a bit different, but I was shocked and struggled with the news at first. I turned to our pastor for guidance, and after much prayer and reflection, my wife and I decided to love and support him no matter what.

At 47, I was passed over for a promotion at work despite my qualifications. The boss gave it to someone younger and less experienced. I felt a deep sense of injustice, but my faith kept me grounded. I decided to approach my church elders about starting a support group for unemployed and underappreciated workers in our community.

At 49, I was devastated when my church's youth pastor was caught embezzling funds, shaking the foundation of our tight-knit community. I had to confront my disappointment in both the pastor and our leadership for letting this happen under our noses. Despite the turmoil, our congregation rallied together, providing moral support for the affected families and nine months of financial continuity for our local charity projects.

compressed_summaries.summary: This person experienced a decade-long job loss at 32, leading to temporary marital strain but also strengthening church community ties. At 45, they deepened their commitment to service through a mission trip, while at 40, they lost contact with their best friend of 25 years due to relocation. At 42, their oldest son came out as gay, initially causing struggle before they decided to support him.

This person experienced a sudden loss of contact with their Black best friend of 25 years due to relocation, missing their shared history and understanding. At 42, they struggled initially but ultimately supported their oldest son coming out as gay, seeking guidance from their pastor. At 49, they were devastated by their church's youth pastor embezzling funds, leading to community turmoil but also unity in supporting affected families and charity projects.

persona_card: This 55-year-old, strongly Republican, extremely conservative Protestant man is deeply devoted to his faith, attending services more than once a week. He believes in traditional moral values and supports a limited role for government in economic redistribution, while also expressing some openness towards outgroups. Despite his individualistic leanings in explaining racial inequality, he holds low trust in institutions, including the government and the legal system. He has struggled with, but ultimately accepted, his son's homosexuality and the embezzlement scandal in his church, demonstrating a capacity for growth and resilience despite his conservative views.
